content.problemStatement:
We completed an example on subplots in the previous module. Let us revisit subplots in the current module as well.

Subplots allow you to create multiple plots within a single figure. This is useful for comparing different datasets or showing various aspects of the same data.

We have populated the subplot code in the IDE - it is a slight variant of what we learnt in the previous module. Let us understand this code better
- fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
   - This line creates a figure with two subplots side by side
   - plt.subplots(1, 2) creates 1 row and 2 columns of subplots
   - (ax1, ax2) are Axes objects, representing each subplot. We get two because we specified 2 columns
- ax1.plot(x, np.sin(x)) - ax1 refers to the first subplot
- ax2.plot(x, np.cos(x)) - ax2 refers to the second subplot

### Task
Create a figure with 3 subplots arranged vertically. \
Plot y = x, y = x^2, and y = x^3 for x values from 0 to 5 in the three subplots respectively. \
You can name each subplot as per your choice.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
